title: git merge vs git rebase
question: What's the difference between merge and rebase?
answer: merge preserves history as a graph and creates a merge commit. rebase rewrites your branch's commits onto another base, producing a linear history.
explanation: Never rebase shared branches — you rewrite history.
codeExample:
git checkout feature
git rebase main
bestPractices: Rebase your local feature branch before opening a PR.
commonMistakes: Rebasing shared branches (main).
interviewTips: Discuss --interactive rebases.

title: reset vs revert vs checkout
question: When do you use reset, revert, and checkout (or restore)?
answer: revert: create a new commit undoing a previous one (safe on shared branches). reset: move HEAD/branch pointer; --soft/--mixed/--hard vary how the working tree changes. checkout/restore: switch or restore files/branches without rewriting history.
explanation: Prefer revert for shared branches; reset for local mistakes.
codeExample:
git revert <sha>
git reset --hard HEAD~1  # danger: destroys uncommitted work
bestPractices: Never reset --hard on shared branches.
commonMistakes: Using reset on published commits.
interviewTips: Explain reflog as a safety net.

title: Stash
question: What does git stash do?
answer: Temporarily shelves uncommitted changes so you can switch context, then restore them later with git stash pop/apply.
explanation: Great for quickly switching to a hotfix branch.
codeExample:
git stash push -m "wip"
# ...work elsewhere...
git stash pop
bestPractices: Use descriptive messages when stashing.
commonMistakes: Losing stashes by forgetting they exist.
interviewTips: Show stashing including untracked files (-u).

title: Cherry-pick
question: When do you use git cherry-pick?
answer: To apply a specific commit from one branch onto another without merging the entire branch.
explanation: Handy for hotfixes and backporting.
codeExample: git cherry-pick <sha>
bestPractices: Prefer cherry-pick for isolated fixes.
commonMistakes: Duplicating commit history without noting it.
interviewTips: Discuss conflict resolution.

title: Branching Strategies
question: Compare GitFlow, GitHub Flow, and Trunk-Based Development.
answer: GitFlow: long-lived develop/main + feature/release/hotfix branches (heavy). GitHub Flow: main + short-lived feature branches merged via PRs (light). Trunk-Based: everyone commits to main behind feature flags, enabling continuous delivery.
explanation: Trunk-based scales best with modern CI/CD.
codeExample:
git checkout -b feature/x
# ... commit, push, PR, merge, delete branch
bestPractices: Keep branches short-lived.
commonMistakes: Long-lived feature branches causing painful merges.
interviewTips: Discuss feature flags for trunk-based.

title: The Three Git Areas
question: Explain the working directory, staging area, and repository.
answer: The working directory holds your actual files. The staging area (index) is a snapshot of what will go into the next commit. The repository (.git) stores committed history permanently. git add moves changes from working dir to staging; git commit moves staging to the repository.
explanation: Understanding these three areas demystifies almost every Git command — add, commit, reset, and diff all operate between them.
codeExample:
git status            # see working dir vs staging
git add file.txt      # working dir -> staging
git commit -m "msg"  # staging -> repository
git diff              # working dir vs staging
git diff --staged     # staging vs last commit
bestPractices: Run git status often to know which area a change is in.
Stage related changes together for clean commits.
commonMistakes: Assuming git add commits the change.
Confusing git diff with git diff --staged.
interviewTips: Draw the three areas and show which command moves data between them.

title: HEAD and Detached HEAD
question: What is HEAD, and what does a detached HEAD state mean?
answer: HEAD is a pointer to the current commit you have checked out — usually it points to a branch, which in turn points to a commit. A detached HEAD occurs when you check out a specific commit or tag directly, so HEAD points at a commit instead of a branch. New commits made in this state aren't attached to any branch and can be lost.
explanation: Detached HEAD is safe for inspecting old states, but you must create a branch to keep any new commits.
codeExample:
git checkout 9fceb02      # detached HEAD at a commit
# make experimental commits...
git switch -c experiment  # save work onto a new branch
git switch main           # reattach HEAD to main
bestPractices: Create a branch before committing in detached HEAD.
Use git switch - to jump back to the previous branch.
commonMistakes: Committing in detached HEAD then losing the work after checkout.
Confusing HEAD with the main branch.
interviewTips: Explain HEAD -> branch -> commit indirection clearly.

title: .gitignore
question: How does .gitignore work and what are its limits?
answer: .gitignore lists glob patterns for untracked files Git should not stage or report. It only affects untracked files — files already tracked continue to be tracked even if they later match a pattern. Patterns can be negated with !, anchored with a leading /, and match directories with a trailing /.
explanation: To stop tracking an already-committed file, you must remove it from the index with git rm --cached in addition to ignoring it.
codeExample:
# .gitignore
node_modules/
*.log
.env
!.env.example

# stop tracking a file already committed
git rm --cached .env
git commit -m "chore: stop tracking .env"
bestPractices: Add secrets and build artifacts to .gitignore early.
Commit a .env.example while ignoring the real .env.
commonMistakes: Expecting .gitignore to untrack already-committed files.
Committing node_modules or secrets before ignoring them.
interviewTips: Mention git rm --cached for retroactively ignoring tracked files.

title: git diff Deep Dive
question: What are the different git diff comparisons?
answer: git diff shows working directory vs staging. git diff --staged (or --cached) shows staging vs the last commit. git diff HEAD shows working dir + staging vs the last commit. git diff branchA..branchB compares two branches, and git diff <sha1> <sha2> compares two commits.
explanation: Choosing the right diff scope is essential for reviewing exactly what will be committed versus what has changed overall.
codeExample:
git diff                 # unstaged changes
git diff --staged        # staged changes
git diff HEAD            # all uncommitted changes
git diff main..feature   # branch comparison
git diff --stat          # summary of changed files
bestPractices: Run git diff --staged before committing to verify content.
Use --stat for a quick high-level overview.
commonMistakes: Forgetting --staged and reviewing the wrong set of changes.
Confusing two-dot and three-dot diff ranges.
interviewTips: Explain the difference between diff, diff --staged, and diff HEAD.

title: git log and Formatting
question: How do you inspect and format commit history with git log?
answer: git log lists commits from newest to oldest. Flags refine the view: --oneline for compact output, --graph for a visual branch topology, --all for every ref, -p for patches, and --pretty=format for custom layouts. You can filter with --author, --since, and -- <path>.
explanation: A well-formed log command is the fastest way to understand project history and locate specific changes.
codeExample:
git log --oneline --graph --decorate --all
git log --pretty=format:"%h %an %ar %s" -5
git log --since="2 weeks ago" --author="Alice"
git log -- path/to/file.ts
bestPractices: Create a git alias for your favourite log format.
Use --graph --all to visualise branch structure.
commonMistakes: Scrolling raw log output instead of using --oneline.
Not filtering by path when tracing a file's history.
interviewTips: Show a custom --pretty=format string with placeholders.

title: Tags and Releases
question: What are Git tags and how do lightweight and annotated tags differ?
answer: Tags mark specific commits, typically release points. A lightweight tag is just a name pointing at a commit. An annotated tag is a full object storing the tagger, date, message, and can be GPG-signed — it is recommended for releases. Tags are not pushed by default; you push them explicitly.
explanation: Annotated tags carry metadata and are the standard for versioned releases like v1.2.0.
codeExample:
git tag v1.2.0                       # lightweight
git tag -a v1.2.0 -m "Release 1.2.0"  # annotated
git push origin v1.2.0               # push one tag
git push origin --tags               # push all tags
git tag -d v1.2.0                    # delete locally
bestPractices: Use annotated, signed tags for releases.
Follow semantic versioning for tag names.
commonMistakes: Forgetting to push tags to the remote.
Using lightweight tags for official releases.
interviewTips: Contrast lightweight vs annotated tags and mention signing.

title: Remotes and Tracking Branches
question: What is a remote and how do tracking branches work?
answer: A remote is a named reference to another copy of the repository (e.g. origin). A remote-tracking branch like origin/main is a local read-only pointer reflecting the remote's state as of the last fetch. A local tracking branch is configured with an upstream so push/pull know the target automatically.
explanation: Tracking branches let git status report ahead/behind counts and let bare git push/pull work without arguments.
codeExample:
git remote -v                         # list remotes
git remote add origin git@host:repo.git
git branch -u origin/main             # set upstream
git push -u origin feature            # push and track
git branch -vv                        # show tracking info
bestPractices: Use -u on first push to establish tracking.
Verify tracking relationships with git branch -vv.
commonMistakes: Confusing origin/main (remote-tracking) with a local branch.
Assuming git pull knows the upstream without configuration.
interviewTips: Explain the role of the remote-tracking ref during fetch.

title: fetch vs pull
question: What is the difference between git fetch and git pull?
answer: git fetch downloads new commits and updates remote-tracking branches (origin/main) but does NOT change your working branch. git pull is fetch followed by an integration step (merge by default, or rebase with --rebase) that updates your current branch.
explanation: Fetch is safe and non-destructive; pull immediately modifies your branch, so understanding the integration step matters.
codeExample:
git fetch origin                 # update refs only
git log HEAD..origin/main        # preview incoming commits
git pull --rebase origin main    # fetch + rebase
git config pull.rebase true      # make rebase the default
bestPractices: Fetch first to review changes before integrating.
Prefer pull --rebase for a linear history.
commonMistakes: Assuming fetch changes your working branch.
Creating noisy merge commits with default pull.
interviewTips: Describe pull as fetch + merge/rebase.

title: Push and --force-with-lease
question: Why prefer git push --force-with-lease over --force?
answer: After rewriting history (rebase, amend), a normal push is rejected because it isn't a fast-forward. --force overwrites the remote unconditionally and can clobber teammates' commits. --force-with-lease only overwrites if the remote is still at the commit you last saw, refusing the push if someone pushed in the meantime.
explanation: --force-with-lease gives you the power of force pushing while protecting against silently discarding others' work.
codeExample:
git rebase -i HEAD~3
# history rewritten, plain push rejected
git push --force-with-lease origin feature
# aborts if origin/feature moved since your last fetch
bestPractices: Always use --force-with-lease instead of --force.
Fetch before force-pushing to refresh your lease.
commonMistakes: Using --force and overwriting a colleague's commits.
Force pushing to shared branches like main.
interviewTips: Explain how the lease check compares remote refs.

title: Resolving Merge Conflicts
question: How do you resolve a merge conflict?
answer: When Git can't auto-merge overlapping changes, it marks the file with conflict markers (<<<<<<<, =======, >>>>>>>). You edit the file to the desired final state, remove the markers, git add the resolved files, then complete the merge with git commit (or git merge --continue). Use git merge --abort to bail out.
explanation: Conflicts are normal; resolving them is about choosing the correct combined result, not just picking one side blindly.
codeExample:
git merge feature
# CONFLICT in app.ts
git status                 # list conflicted files
# edit file, remove <<<<<<< ======= >>>>>>> markers
git add app.ts
git commit                 # finalise the merge
# or: git merge --abort
bestPractices: Pull/rebase frequently to keep conflicts small.
Use a merge tool or IDE for complex conflicts.
commonMistakes: Leaving conflict markers in the committed file.
Blindly accepting one side and losing changes.
interviewTips: Explain each conflict marker section and the resolution flow.

title: Interactive Rebase and Squash
question: How do you clean up commits with interactive rebase?
answer: git rebase -i lets you reorder, edit, drop, reword, and combine commits before sharing them. Marking commits as squash (s) or fixup (f) merges them into the preceding commit, producing a tidy, meaningful history. Because it rewrites history, only do it on unshared branches.
explanation: Interactive rebase is the primary tool for curating a clean, reviewable commit history before opening a PR.
codeExample:
git rebase -i HEAD~4
# in the editor:
# pick a1b2c3 Add feature
# squash d4e5f6 Fix typo
# fixup 7g8h9i WIP
# reword ...
# save to rewrite the last 4 commits
bestPractices: Squash noise commits before opening a PR.
Only interactive-rebase local, unpushed commits.
commonMistakes: Rebasing shared branches and forcing teammates to recover.
Confusing squash (keeps messages) with fixup (discards them).
interviewTips: Differentiate pick, squash, fixup, reword, and drop.

title: Recovering with reflog
question: How can git reflog rescue lost commits?
answer: git reflog records every movement of HEAD (and branch tips) locally — including commits orphaned by reset --hard, a bad rebase, or a deleted branch. You find the desired prior state's hash in the reflog and restore it with git reset --hard or by creating a branch from it.
explanation: Because reflog tracks where HEAD has been, almost nothing is truly lost until Git garbage-collects unreachable objects (typically 30–90 days).
codeExample:
git reflog
# 9fceb02 HEAD@{2}: commit: important work
# a1b2c3d HEAD@{1}: reset: moving to HEAD~3
git reset --hard HEAD@{2}      # restore that state
# or recover a deleted branch:
git branch recovered a1b2c3d
bestPractices: Check reflog before assuming work is lost.
Create a branch from the recovered hash to keep it.
commonMistakes: Panicking after reset --hard instead of checking reflog.
Assuming reflog is shared — it is local only.
interviewTips: Position reflog as the safety net behind reset and rebase.

title: Finding Bugs with git bisect
question: How does git bisect help locate a regression?
answer: git bisect performs a binary search through history to find the commit that introduced a bug. You mark a known-bad commit and a known-good commit; Git checks out the midpoint repeatedly and you mark each good or bad until it isolates the first bad commit. It can be automated with a test script.
explanation: Bisect turns an O(n) hunt through commits into O(log n), dramatically speeding up regression hunting on large histories.
codeExample:
git bisect start
git bisect bad                 # current commit is broken
git bisect good v1.0           # this tag worked
# test each checkout, mark good/bad...
git bisect run npm test        # automate it
git bisect reset               # finish
bestPractices: Automate bisect with a reliable test via git bisect run.
Always git bisect reset when finished.
commonMistakes: Marking commits good/bad incorrectly and misleading the search.
Forgetting to reset, leaving a detached HEAD.
interviewTips: Explain the binary-search complexity advantage.

title: git blame
question: What does git blame tell you and how is it used well?
answer: git blame annotates each line of a file with the commit, author, and date that last changed it, helping you understand why a line exists and who to ask. Options like -L limit line ranges, and -w or -M/-C ignore whitespace and detect moved/copied code for more accurate attribution.
explanation: Blame is about understanding context and history, not assigning fault — pair it with the referenced commit to read the full rationale.
codeExample:
git blame -L 40,60 src/app.ts
git blame -w -M src/app.ts      # ignore whitespace, detect moves
git show <sha>                  # read the full commit context
bestPractices: Use -w to ignore whitespace-only reformatting noise.
Follow up with git show to read the commit's reasoning.
commonMistakes: Blaming a formatting commit instead of the real author.
Treating blame as fault-finding rather than context.
interviewTips: Mention -L, -w, and -M/-C for accurate attribution.

title: Amend and commit --fixup
question: How do amend and --fixup adjust recent commits?
answer: git commit --amend replaces the most recent commit, letting you fix its message or add staged changes. git commit --fixup=<sha> creates a specially-marked commit targeting an older commit; a later git rebase -i --autosquash automatically positions and squashes it into that target. Both rewrite history, so avoid on pushed commits.
explanation: --amend fixes the last commit immediately, while --fixup + autosquash cleanly folds a fix into an earlier commit during rebase.
codeExample:
git commit --amend -m "fix: corrected message"
# fold a fix into an older commit:
git add patch.ts
git commit --fixup=a1b2c3d
git rebase -i --autosquash a1b2c3d~1
bestPractices: Use --fixup + --autosquash to target older commits cleanly.
Only amend commits that haven't been pushed.
commonMistakes: Amending a pushed commit and needing a force push.
Manually reordering fixups instead of using --autosquash.
interviewTips: Contrast --amend (last commit) with --fixup (any prior commit).
